chunks.text: Richard S. Muller is Professor Emeritus at UC Berkeley with contact information including office at 568 Cory Hall, phone 510-642-0614, fax 643-6690, email muller@berkeley.edu, and office hours by email appointment only, best time Tuesdays 2:30-4:30. He has a personal homepage, publications, dissertations, and research support listed, with administrative contact Leslie Goldstein at 765 Soda, phone 3-2469, email lgolds@berkeley.edu.
chunks.question: What is Richard Muller's position and contact details?

chunks.text: Richard Muller received a Mechanical Engineer degree from Stevens Institute of Technology in 1955, and M.S. and Ph.D. in Electrical Engineering and Physics from Caltech in 1957 and 1962. He worked at Hughes Aircraft Company from 1955 to 1962, then joined UC Berkeley's Electrical Engineering faculty in 1962. His early research on integrated-circuit physics led to co-authoring 'Device Electronics for Integrated Circuits' with Theodore Kamins, first published in 1977 with editions in 1986 and 2002.
chunks.question: What are Muller's education and early career highlights?
chunks.answer: Degrees from Stevens and Caltech, worked at Hughes.

chunks.text: In the late 1970s, Muller shifted focus to microelectromechanical systems (MEMS) and co-founded the Berkeley Sensor & Actuator Center (BSAC) with Richard White in 1986. He proposed the IEEE/ASME Journal of Microelectromechanical Systems in 1990, which started in 1991. He has over 300 research papers, 19 patents, and awards including NATO and Fulbright Fellowships, Humboldt Award, UC Berkeley Citation, Stevens Renaissance Award, Transducers Career Achievement Award, IEEE Cledo Brunetti Award, and IEEE Millennium Medal.
chunks.question: What did Muller contribute to MEMS and what awards has he received?
chunks.answer: Co-founded BSAC and proposed JMEMS journal.

chunks.text: Muller is a member of the National Academy of Engineering, IEEE Life Fellow, and served as an IEEE Distinguished Lecturer. His research areas include Integrated Circuits, Solid-State Devices, Optoelectronics, Microsystem technologies, Sensors and actuators, and Microelectromechanical systems. Selected publications cover topics like silicon photonic cross-connects, MEMS interferometers, optical switches, and device electronics, with works from 2003 to 2019.
chunks.question: What are Muller's professional memberships and research areas?
chunks.answer: NAE member, IEEE Fellow, focuses on MEMS.

chunks.text: Selected honors and awards include IEEE James Clerk Maxwell Medal (2013), IEEE EDS Distinguished Service Award (2007), IEEE Millennium Medal (2000), IEEE Cledo Brunetti Award (1998), Stevens Renaissance Award (1995), Berkeley Citation (1994), National Academy of Engineering Member (1992), IEEE Fellow (1988), Fulbright Scholar (1982), and NATO Fellow (1968).
chunks.question: What major awards has Richard Muller earned?
chunks.answer: IEEE Maxwell Medal and NAE membership.
